author: @scottaseigel5715
text:
Let's see, GAC (AKA China) via Toyota wants to sell cars in the United States powered by the single most toxic gas moved routinely on the American railroads. What could be the harm?  I teach chemistry and this issue (INSANITY) simply can't be ignored. Even Fritz Haber knew how dangerous this stuff was and tried warning people!  But hey, the upside is saving the planet, right? NO THAT'S WRONG! I guess toxically wiping out communities (including basically every organism in the area of any leaks) in order to play the carbon neutrality game is a worthwhile risk, right? Heck, if true, why not develop "nuclear" Stirling engine based cars using radioisotope thermoelectric generator (RTG)--at least such (dirty) devices would have decent weight-to-efficiency AND they're carbon neutral too. They would also be wickedly dangerous. 

Even merely fueling this vehicle or transfering the fuel from the truck or train at filling stations would be a high level hazardous material handling operation (and very likely to end people). Only 1 gram of ammonia (about 1ml or 1cc) is enough to wipe out everyone in a car within seconds. A comparably sized fuel tank will only give us 1/3 the range of a fossil fuel, so we're stuck either having much larger tanks (say 150 gallons for a normal car)--that, OR fueling more often. Switching the "fuel" over to to ammonia water would triple that tank AGAIN (and greatly increase the weight). All that fuss still assumes the solution to be temperature stablized (because when it warms up that horrible ammonia gas is released). This CAN be managed by using a less concentrated solution, which adds still more weight and volume. This is profoundly impractical. 

Finally, we have to look at the non-trivial (and NON-zero emmisions) energy hungry process of ammonia productionn which requires significant heat and pressure--and the cherry on top: it's dangerous too! 

For Toyota to be merely "looking at this" is frankly hard to believe unless somebody is getting paid off. Ammonia-based appliances (mainly air conditioning) and other systems were eliminated MANY years ago in favor of safer alternatives because even pinhole leaks were removing entire families; maybe somebody's nostalgic for the bad old days. 

WE NEED TO HALT THIS MISGUIDED IDEA BEFORE SOME WELL-INTENTIONED (BUT HORRIBLY MISINFORMED) CONSUMERS PUT AN END TO THEMSELVES AND PERHAPS THEIR NEIGHBORHOODS!

If the fuel/air mixture is correct, there should be no NOx - a clean burn of ammonia results in just water and inert nitrogen gas. So the trick is to get the mixture spot on; fortunately modern fuel injection systems are very good at this. Getting it wrong could even result in N2O (nitrous oxide), which is hundreds of times worse than CO2 as a greenhouse gas.
The point about current ammonia production methods is exactly right - for this to work as a means of tackling greenhouse gas emissions, we need a clean way of producing ammonia. I'm thinking floating offshore factories located next to offshore wind turbines that provide the power when the wind blows (and when it doesn't, they just sit idle and wait).

author: @lloydevans2900
text:
It is possible to make pure ammonia burn in air without mixing it with another fuel - you just need a catalyst. Nitric acid production plants do this all the time - they take the ammonia produced by the Haber-Bosch process and literally burn it using a platinum gauze catalyst. I remember doing a small scale demonstration of this in a fume cupboard in a school chemistry lesson - this was just a flask with some concentrated ammonia solution on a hotplate, heated enough to drive some of the ammonia out of solution, and then a pre-heated platinum wire held above the neck of the flask to ignite the ammonia gas coming out. The incredible amount of heat generated by this reaction is used at nitric acid production plants to drive a recovery boiler and steam turbogenerators, making enough power to run the plant and usually a bit of excess which is sold to the electricity grid. Of course for nitric acid production they want to make nitrogen oxides on purpose, since they then react these with water to make the nitric acid.
However, it is possible to burn ammonia catalytically in the same way without producing nitrogen oxides: You can either inject more ammonia than the available oxygen will burn, which means that any nitrogen oxides which are produced during combustion are reduced back to nitrogen gas by the excess ammonia. Or you do staged combustion, where you inject more ammonia into the exhaust at a point where it it still hot enough for the nitrogen oxide reduction reaction to work, giving the same result overall.
The staged combustion method obviously isn't of much use to an internal combustion engine, since the energy produced by the second stage is wasted. But this is not a problem for external combustion engines, which large scale power plants still use: For boilers making steam to drive turbogenerators, the staged combustion method with ammonia is quite well suited, since you just add a second combustion zone to the boiler, heating the water in two stages. Or you can use the second stage of ammonia combustion for the steam superheating zone of the boiler, which would be ideal for producing high pressure dry steam, which is the best sort for efficient steam turbines.

agreed - it makes PERFECT sense to power one of those turbines that comes on to supplement the grid now and then with it... and makes ZERO sense to use it to power mobile objects - forklifts, cars, trucks, etc. for the simple reason that an ammonia leak (of the pure stuff) is MUCH scarier than a gas leak. A gas leak can slowly build up and cause an explosion, but it doesn't immediately disable you... if you're in a warehouse and an ammonia line springs a leak, you might be unconscious before you can get to the door.
I worked briefly at a food plant that had an old ammonia freezer system - to freeze "pastys" (a Finnish inspired Hot Pocket basically); and that whole system stank of ammonia even when it was turned "off" - i.e. tiny little cracks in the metal were more than enough for some of the ammonia to seep through, even though the only ammonia in it at that point was a small amount in gaseous state in a few pipes running down the length of it.

author: @Tjalve70
text:
0:30 The FAA's definition of Space, or rather the Karman Line, is 50 miles, or about 80 km.
The FAI's definition of the Karman Line, is 100 km.
And while I am generally a metric guy, in this case I would say the Americans' definition seems to be more correct.

replies.author: @rogerphelps9939
replies.text: They only fiddled it to allow Virgin Galactic to claim that passengers on its underpowered "spaceships"  have received their astronauts' wings.

replies.author: @Tjalve70
replies.text:
@rogerphelps9939 No. That is simply not true.

The Karman Line is not just arbitrarily chosen. It is the height above which an object will remain in orbit for quite some time.
If ANY part of the orbit drops below the Karman Line, then the orbit will start to decay rapidly. The orbit will become circular (if it wasn't already), and then the object will deorbit within a few orbits.

Due to the metric system, internationally 100 km was chosen because it's a nice, round number.
And due to the imperial system, USA chose 50 miles, because it is likewise a fairly round number.

But 50 miles is ACTUALLY a more correct representation. Since an object CAN stay in orbit for a while between 50 miles and 100 km.

But even ignoring that, it is a FACT that FAA's definition of the Karman Line is 50 miles. And it has been so all throughout the Space Age, and has NOTHING to do with Virgin Galactic.

replies.author: @rogerphelps9939
replies.text: This is just splitting hairs. Something at 50 miles will come down a bit faster than the same thing at 100km but the difference is nort a lot.  Nothing has been launched into these orbits to test it. anyway  The FAI version will prevail over American exceptionalism. Anyway the original Virgin Galactic aim was to gtet to 100km and they failed. @Tjalve70

replies.author: @Tjalve70
replies.text:
@rogerphelps9939 It's not really splitting hairs, no. Because you seem to misunderstand what I am saying.

What I am saying is that an object that is orbiting at a height of 90 km is going to stay in orbit for days, or maybe weeks. While an object that is orbiting at a height of 70 km will probably only stay up for a few orbits, and fall down in less than a year.
And THAT is the definition of the Karman Line.

An object orbiting ABOVE he Karman Line is essentially in space, and could boost its orbit if it has fuel. While an object orbiting below the Karman Line is in the process of falling down.

Not to mention that your claim that the USA lowered their Karman Line in order to adapt to Virgin Galactic IS A LIE. Since that is the definition they have used since the start of the Space Age.

replies.author: @Greenammonianews
replies.text:
Don't assume bad intentions.

Fuel is about 75% of the energy consumed in North America and grid is about 25%.
  
Transport trucks, ships, and trains require far more power than batteries can provide. In Canada these together make up about 50% of the fuel usage. Light trucks use 50% more fuel than cars - I don't see the cyber truck or lightning really denting that market either. So realistically, your battery solution might offset 15-20% of the fuel IF there is massive adoption.

Pursuing green fuel makes a lot of sense.

Green fuel can be made separate from the grid. The demand for power in Wyoming is not huge but there is an incredible amount of wind. Expanding the grid does not make a lot of sense but stand-alone green fuel production probably does. This means green fuel can be produced in one region and shipped to another.  India, Australia, Denmark, Newfoundland and more are currently building green ammonia production and export facilities. 

And of course... electric cars are not green until they hit massive miles, they don't work in the cold, they rely on materials mined from all corners of the world that could become incredibly expensive overnight if x-country invades y-country. 


The current figures look like green ammonia can be produced for far less than the cost of gas. 100% green fuel for less than people pay at the pump today. That is something we should ALL celebrate.

Specifically, 300 parts per million (ppm) or higher are considered immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH).... Even if the car was leaking a small amount of ammonia, it could be devastating or deadly to passengers. A car accident wouldn't just potentially kill the people in the car, but anyone walking near the vehicle, and anyone within hundreds of feet of the vehicle. It would be a nightmare. Imagine, the delays trying to get anyone to help someone in any sort of car accident with that level of risk. It would take highly specialized training just to "work" on the car. as well.

replies.author: @sailingoctopus1

No EVs are not 'done'. Sales are still increasing globally, just not as fast as they were before rising interest rates and concerns about the cost of living caused a slowdown in all car sales.

With the imminent launch of next generation EVs with semi-solid-state batteries, like the 150kWh WeLion battery that is set to be made available in all Nio models, the last remaining reasons for favouring ICE over EV are about to disappear (unless you consider the noise/sound of an ICE to be integral with the 'soul' of a car, like some people claim).

As with any maturing technology, the price of EVs are coming down (relative to comparable ICE), so sales are pretty much guaranteed to increase still further as interest rates and electricity prices come down. The price of Lithium batteries dropped to a tenth of their 2010 price over the years to 2020 and after a brief plateauing are now back on their downward trend. Much of the extraordinary R&D and retooling costs of EV manufacture have been fully amortized, so it is only a question of time before competitive forces drive EV prices down. We are already seeing the effects of this, with companies like MG and BYD, and Stellantis are showing signs of following suit with the pricing of the Citroën e-C3.

Anyone that thinks EVs are 'done' are deluding themselves.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: @vitordelima  It's not pure water, it needs to be a strong base. First of all it needs to dissolve the passivation layer off the aluminum and expose the pure metal. Then the Al needs to displace another, less reactive ion, to release the hydrogen in the water, forming aluminum hydroxide as a bypropduct. The aluminum hydroxide can be recycled, as that's the next-to-last step in the refining process, it just takes a lot of electricity to get the metal back. Note, strong acids can also produce hydrogen by reacting with aluminum, but it results in a salt like aluminum chloride, rather than the easily recycled hydroxide form.

replies.author: @Greenammonianews
replies.text:
Basically, hydrogen sucks. 

Compressing hydrogen uses a lot of energy. Then you have hydrogen at 10,000 psi which is basically a bomb. Also, 10,000 psi hydrogen has about 1/3 the energy of ammonia per liter.

You are better off using the energy to make ammonia than compressing hydrogen and trying to handle insanely high pressure in every following step.

It makes practical / financial / energy sense to use ammonia.


Any argument against ammonia, say safety, is outweighed by the alternative, e.g. hydrogen at 10,000 psi vs ammonia at 10 psi.

replies.author: @zinaustro
replies.text: Yes, easier and cheaper to store and handle are the main advantages over H2. However, a Japanese lab has found that perovskytes will store ammonia at ambient temp and pressure. The material has to be heated to about 100F to release the stored ammonia. All energy has costs and drawbacks. They are less obvious with petroleum products because they're so ingrained in our lives.

author: @haddow777
text:
Unfortubately, Ammonia, like Hydrogen, is mostly greenwashing. While there are green ways that could produce it, there are a lot of challenges, like cost, that make it highly unlikely to be green in reality.

The oil and gas industry has beeb pushing Hydrogen hard as a green alternative, because 98% is made using fossil fuels. The oil industry would be like the wizard of Oz hiding behind a Hydrogen curtain pretending to be scaling back production when in reality a Hydrogen based economy would require them to scale up production.

Problems with infrastructure, logistics, and the fact that Hydrogen is itself a greenhouse gas have put a kink in those plans. So, now they are pushing Ammonia. Ammonia production, while existing and well established, also accounts for something like 2% of global greenhouse emissions. It is anything bit emission free. Again, while there is promising technology promising green Ammonia, it is not production ready and scalability may be a serious issue.

Add to that reports shpw that a number of car companies seem tp be using it as an additive to gas and not as a fuel itself.

I love the channel. It does sound like a very interesting tech. Still, when something seems to have such strong support without a lot of info out about it, it seems to be a steong indicator that the oil and gas money is greasing the wheels and putting out PR.

In fact, it really seems like a lot of channels recently sprung into existance to make everyone beleive a lot of negative things about EVs. Lots of money moving around right now to sway oppinions.

It was pretty cool to hear about those planes though.

replies.author: @haddow777
replies.text:
@Greenammonianews lol  oh ya. And Santa is real. Give me a break. I've been looking into Amonia for a while now and been listening to experts who work in the production side of things. I haven't heard anything that is actually production ready that is close to those numbers. Especially the Nitrogen extracting side of the system. The Hydrogen is easier to break down than the Nitrogen by far.

More, it would be interesting if they account for the materials that go into the equipment into the cost. One of the major problems with many green systems with Hydrogen and such is that they tend to use very rare materials and so it will never scale well.

Beyond that, I don't find your figure of %0.90/L very specific. Is that supposed be $0.90/L of Ammonia, or the amount of Amonia it would take to be equivalent to a Liter of Diesel. Ammonia was looked at as a fuel source a long time ago and was discarded as a viable fuel source back then because it was more expensive and not nearly as energy dense as fossil fuels.

As well, which method of using Ammonia are you thinking will happen? An Ammonia cell that generates electricity like a Hydrogen cell or by combustion? I've heard of R&D in Ammonia cells, but that it's no where near viable for production. Combustion has a whole host of problems. A big one is that Ammonia provides so little power in combustion that pretty much any operation looking at producing Ammonia combustion engines are planning for it to be more of a fuel additive than the fuel itself. Typically mixing no more than 40% of a tanks capacity worth of Ammonia in with a fossil fuel like gas.

Of course, it doesn't matter a lot because at the scales necessary to displace fossil fuels, pretty much any expert I've seen discuss the matter universally state that the vast majority of tbe produced amount will come from fossil fuel production.

replies.author: @stephenkeller8208
replies.text:
ICE vehicles have improved tremendously even in the past 30-40 years, especially gas engines. They will never be green or eco-friendly but to say that are the same as 100 years ago is a flat out lie. 

No more carbeurators, we have vastly more fuel efficient and more reliable fuel injection systems. We have small efficient V6 and I4 engines making the same or more power as the old school V8 and L6 engines twice the size.

replies.author: @mitchhilger5394
replies.text: The fuel economy of cars has dramatically increased, but much of that is due to weight reduction and better body shapes to minimize drag. The engine has adapted via computer controls to varying conditions and burn, but a properly tuned older engine is about the same efficiency give or take 10%. The engine itself has not doubled in efficiency rated against a 60’s or 70’s engine.  So I still believe no significant efficiency gains have been achieved.   In a similar way that incandescent lights gave off a significant amount of their energy via heat (Otto cycle - thermo course) an LED is significantly more efficient. So I see EVs as a huge advantage with the current weak link as the power source, but we are early in the game.

author: @Ammoniummetavanadate
text:
I worked on this for a while, green Haber-Bosch where we sourced the hydrogen from splitting water.

Absolutely massive pain to make it remotely economical. You get all the issues with making hydrogen with high pressure and temperature conditions for making the ammonia and the massive issue of getting rid of absolutely all of the water and oxygen so the catalyst does not die.

Cool on paper, maybe someone makes it work, we burned up like $250K in grants and didn't get very far.

With traditional HB you have the carbon from the natural gas to get rid of your water, oxygen, and any CO.

Probably the closest you will get at scale is a natural gas process with a point capture system to sink the CO2.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text:
I agree. There are numerous benefits to this idea of hybrid vehicles over pure EV. The first is as you mentioned, you can do short runs on pure battery power, and if that runs out, burn fuel instead. However, another reason to go hybrid is that it keeps competition going between the utility plant and the gas fuel production industry... because if there is no competition, you know the prices will escalate faster, and another good reason is that with pure EV, the US and most other countries simply do not have a utility production grid that can handle the EV load... specifically, 1 car uses about 1/3 to 1/2 of an average house's worth of electricity... (varies depending on house, car and distance). The general point tho, is that if a utility services 10,000 homes, it simply cannot supply 15,000 homes... and if 1 house has 1 or 2 cars... it would be like adding an extra 1/2 of a house for each person driving. 
So, hybrid cars would allow the grid to be updated, while simultaneously allowing cars to save fuel.
